coredata.dc:title: An ammonia-sensitive fluorescence sensor based on polyvinyl alcohol-graphene quantum dots/halloysite nanotubes hybrid film for monitoring fish freshness
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jiaran
Yu, Jie
Yin, Hao
Jia, Zhixin
Shi, Ce
Yue, Yuntao
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent hybrid film composed of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) loaded on halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) (N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite) as a sensitive element and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as a film-forming matrix was designed for freshness detection. The PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film exhibited significantly enhanced fluorescence attributed to the loading of N-GQDs onto the surface of HNTs through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, effectively reducing their aggregation. The fluorescence of the hybrid film could be quenched by ammonia via photoinduced electron transfer (PET), with good linearity in the range of 20 ppm to 500 ppm ammonia and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.63 ppm. In addition, the hybrid film was applied to monitor the freshness of seawater fish and freshwater fish stored at refrigeration and room temperature to evaluate the practicality of this approach. The developed hybrid film showed promise for nondestructive and on-site monitoring of fish spoilage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Halloysite nanotubes
ammonia
Freshness
Fluorescence film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanical properties, moisture content (MC) and water contact angle (WCA)
Sensing performance of the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film
Mechanical properties, MC, WVP and WCA
Sensing performance of the hybrid film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images (inset: particle size distribution of N-GQDs), (b) FT-IR spectrum, XPS spectrum of (c) survey, (d) C1s, (e) N1s, and (f) O1s of N-GQDs/NHTs.
SEM image of (a) (c) the surface and (b) (d) cross-section of PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film; (e) FTIR spectra; (f) XRD spectra of PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs, PVA-HNTs, PVA-N-GQDs and PVA.

(a) The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film and PVA-N-GQDs film (insert: photos of PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film (i) and PVA-N-GQDs film (ii) under sunlight and 365 nm UV light); (b) fluorescence intensity of GQDs/HNTs at different pH; (c) the fluorescence intensity of hybrid films fabricated with different N-GQDs/HNTs (Table S1); (d) fluorescence spectra of the hybrid film exposed in different concentrations of ammonia (0, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400 and 500, unit: ppm) (insert: photos of hybrid film without and with ammonia); (e) calibration curves of F0/F versus ammonia; (f) fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N-GQDs/HNTs without and with ammonia.

TVB-N and TAC for samples of bighead carp at (a) 25 °C in 24 h and (c) 0 °C in 16 d, the calibration curves of the fluorescence intensity and TVB-N values at (b) 25 °C and (d) 0 °C, and the application of the hybrid films on the detection of bighead carp at (e) 25 °C and (f) 0 °C.
(a) The synthesis process of PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film; (b) the fluorescence quenching mechanism; (c) and the application in freshness detection of fish.
Mechanical properties, MC, and WCA of PVA, PVA-HNTs, PVA-N-GQDs and PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values are means ± standard deviation. Treatments followed by the different letters within the same column are significantly different (P < 0.05).

head.ce:title: An ammonia-sensitive fluorescence sensor based on polyvinyl alcohol-graphene quantum dots/halloysite nanotubes hybrid film for monitoring fish freshness

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Academy of Agriculture and Forestry Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent hybrid film composed of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) loaded on halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) (N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite) as a sensitive element and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as a film-forming matrix was designed for freshness detection. The PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film exhibited significantly enhanced fluorescence attributed to the loading of N-GQDs onto the surface of HNTs through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, effectively reducing their aggregation. The fluorescence of the hybrid film could be quenched by ammonia via photoinduced electron transfer (PET), with good linearity in the range of 20 ppm to 500 ppm ammonia and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.63 ppm. In addition, the hybrid film was applied to monitor the freshness of seawater fish and freshwater fish stored at refrigeration and room temperature to evaluate the practicality of this approach. The developed hybrid film showed promise for nondestructive and on-site monitoring of fish spoilage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fish are an important component of the daily diets of humans. According to 2021 statistics from the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO, 2021), the global per capita annual consumption of fish was 14.82 kg. However, postharvest fish are highly susceptible to spoilage due to microbial decomposition, enzymatic reactions, and other autolytic chemical processes. Spoiled fish not only cause economic losses but also put consumers at increased risk of contracting foodborne diseases. Therefore, evaluating the freshness of fish to prevent spoilage during storage and transportation is highly important.

Novel methods for assessing fish freshness, including electrochemical (Senapati & Sahu, 2020), optical (Zhang, Wang, Xu, Shi, & Yang, 2022), and biosensing (Sriramulu et al., 2024) methods, are gradually replacing physical-chemical experimental analyses and microbiological analyses to achieve simplified, rapid and miniaturized detection. Previously, our team reported the development of an amine-sensitive fluorescent sensor based on an InP/ZnS QDs‑sodium rhodizonate (SR) system for the fluorescence-visualized determination of fish freshness (Zhang et al., 2022). Nevertheless, the fluorescent dye, namely, InP/ZnS QDs in solution, was susceptible to photobleaching when exposed to harsh excitation sources, resulting in poor long-term photostability. Moreover, the practical application of liquid fluorescent sensors and their integration with electrodevices are challenging.

Solid fluorescent films are increasingly being proposed to overcome the limitations of liquid fluorescent sensors. However, due to the induced aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect, the emission intensity of most fluorescent dyes is reduced in aggregated or solid substrates (Feng, Kwok, Tang, & Liu, 2017), resulting in poor sensitivity of the fluorescent films. The most common method for constructing solid-state fluorescent films is to disperse fluorescent dyes in various solid matrices, including colloidal matrices, polymer chains, and mesoporous materials, to overcome the ACQ effect (Wang et al., 2023). Due to the anchoring and diluting effect on fluorescence dyes of solid substrates, their dispersion at the molecular scale is enhanced, thus reducing their aggregation.

Carbon-based quantum dots (QDs) have emerged as promising candidates for the construction of solid fluorescent films for freshness monitoring due to their low cytotoxicity and excellent fluorescence properties (Ezati, Khan, Rhim, Kim, & Molaei, 2023). Most carbon-based QDs fluorescent sensors for monitoring freshness have been designed to be sensitive to pH, which is affected by the accumulation of volatile amines in package headspace (Ezati et al., 2023; Khan, Ezati, Rhim, Kim, & Molaei, 2023; Song et al., 2024). However, the volatile compounds generated during fish spoilage are diverse and include acidic volatile compounds such as hydrogen sulfide, thiols, and carbon dioxide, in addition to amines. Therefore, concerns have been raised about potential interference of pH-sensitive fluorescent films from the acidic volatile compounds generated during fish spoilage. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as carbon-based QDs, are important emerging materials in the field of food safety due to their excellent optical, electronic and biocompatibility properties (Qiu et al., 2022). In addition, it is easy to functionalize the surface groups on GQDs by heteroatom doping to reduce the impact of pH on the emission.

In this study, nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) were synthesized and loaded on the surface of halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) to improve their fluorescence properties. An ammonia-sensitive fluorescent sensor was developed based on a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film. The microstructure, optical properties, sensing performance, mechanical properties, moisture content (MC) and water contact angle (WCA) of the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film were investigated. A mechanism to explain the fluorescence quenching of the N-GQDs/HNT composite by ammonia was proposed. Finally, the performance of the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film in monitoring fish freshness was evaluated. In comparison to previously reports, our sensor possesses several advantages: (1) The hollow tubular structure of halloysite provides active loading sites for N-GQDs, which enhances the dispersion of N-GQDs and inhibits the ACQ effect, resulting in a sensor with high sensitivity. (2) The proposed hybrid film is insensitive to pH, which allows more accurate results to be obtained by eliminating interference from the acidic volatile compounds generated during fish spoilage. To our knowledge, there have been no reports on the use of GQDs-loaded HNTs for the determination of fish freshness.

Ethanol, boric acid, methyl red, methylene blue, magnesium oxide, dimethylamine (DMA), trimethylamine (TMA), acetic acid, hydrochloric acid, and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were all purchased from Shanghai Yuan Ye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Salicylic acid and pyridine-2,6-dicarboxylic acid were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) were sourced from Shanxi, China. Gases such as NH3, CO2, H2S, NO and NO2 were obtained from Beijing Xinyang Spectral Chemical Co., Ltd. Distilled water with a conductivity of 1.37 μs/cm and a resistivity of 0.73 MΩ/cm was purchased from A.S. Watson (Hong Kong, China). All chemical reagents used were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

N-GQDs were synthesized according to previous reports with some improvements (Zhu et al., 2017). Briefly, a mixture of salicylic acid (100 nM, 500 mL) and 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid at a molar ratio of 10 to 1 was exposed to UV light at 50 °C for 2 h and then rapidly cooled to 5 °C to obtain a pale-yellow N-N-GQDs solution.

To remove impurities from the inner lumens of the HNTs, etching was performed by following previously reported methods (Surya, Masa, Ismail, & Hayeemasae, 2020). Briefly, 10 g of HNTs were added to 1000 mL of sulfuric acid (1.0 M) and stirred at 60 °C for 48 h. Subsequently, the etched HNTs were washed with deionized water until a neutral pH was reached. Finally, the sample was dried at 35 °C and powdered.

N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites were prepared according to the procedure of Lin et al. (Lin, Feng, He, Ding, & Liu, 2023). Multiple sets of nanocomposites with different quantities of HNTs and N-GQDs were prepared, and their fluorescence properties were studied. Specifically, etched HNT powder (5 mg, 10 mg and 15 mg) was individually added to 1 mL, 2 mL and 3 mL of an N-GQDs solution (1.125 mg/mL, pH = 7), and these solutions were mixed and then freeze-dried using a vacuum freeze dryer (Huaxing Technology, LGJ-18, Beijing, China) to obtain powders of N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites.

The films were prepared using the tape-casting method. Briefly, the various masses of N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite powders obtained in Section 2.2.1 were added to 10 mL of distilled water to prepare the suspension solution. This solution was then added to 10 mL of a 4% PVA solution and shaken at 80 °C for 30 min. The masses of the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite powders are presented in Table S1. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a culture dish and dried at 45 °C. The resulting PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid films were then peeled off and cut into several strips measuring 1 × 2 cm for further experiments.

Additionally, films containing pure HNTs and N-GQDs were prepared as controls. The sample numbers and their corresponding N-GQDs/HNTs contents are shown in Table S1. The procedure for film preparation is depicted in Scheme 1 .

The N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites were observed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL, JEM-2100, Akishima-shi, Japan), and the surface and cross-sectional morphologies of the films were observed via field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Hitachi Co. Ltd., Hitachi S-4800, Tokyo, Japan).

Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy was performed at wavelengths ranging from 4000 to 800 cm−1 for 32 scans with a resolution of 4 cm−1 by means of an in situ FTIR spectroscopy system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd.; Nicolet 6700; Waltham, USA).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by using an XPS spectrometer (JEOL Co., Ltd.; D/MAX; Akishima-shi, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) at a rate of 1° (2θ)/min over the range of 2° to 70° and at a voltage of 40 kV and current of 40 mA.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a Mg K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer at 14 kV and 250 W (Physical Electronics Co. Ltd., PHI 5300, Feldkirchen, Germany).

A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., Edinburgh FS5, Livingston, UK) was used to record the fluorescence spectra of the films at an excitation wavelength (λex) of 350 nm and an emission wavelength (λem) of 440 nm, with slits of 3 nm and an integration time of 0.1 s.
Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were acquired by scanning over the wavelength range of 190–600 nm with a step of 2 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hach Company, DR6000, Loveland, USA).

To investigate the optimal fluorescence properties, the experimental conditions of pH and N-GQDs/HNTs content in the hybrid films were studied. The fluorescence intensities of the N-GQDs/HNTs composites at pH values ranging from 4 to 11 were recorded. The fluorescence intensities of the hybrid films fabricated with different N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites, as listed in Table S1, were recorded to determine the optimal N-GQDs/HNTs content in the hybrid film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties, moisture content (MC) and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para: The thickness of the films was measured at 3 locations randomly selected on the film samples using a spiral micrometer (accuracy of 0.01 mm). The tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) were measured according to the method proved by China standard (Singh, Nwabor, Syukri, & Voravuthikunchai, 2021) by means of a texture analyzer (Stable Micro System, TA. XT plus C, Godalming, UK). The MC of the film samples was measured according to previous methods (Singh et al., 2021). The WCA was measured by means of the sessile drop method using a contact angle goniometer (KINO Industrial Co., Ltd., KINO SL250, Boston, USA) according to the procedure described in a previous study (Grzebieniarz et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film
ce:section.ce:para: Various concentrations of ammonia blended with argon were delivered to the hybrid films within an air chamber using mass flow controllers (Beijing Accurate Technology Co., ACU20FD, Beijing, China) at a constant flow rate of 1000 sccm for 3 min, and fluorescence spectra were recorded (λex = 350nm). Subsequently, calibration curves were plotted to correlate the fluorescence intensity (λem = 440nm) with the concentration of ammonia.

The stability, selectivity and reversibility of the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs films were investigated to evaluate their sensing performance. The fluorescence intensity was recorded every day for one week at room temperature to monitor the sensor stability. Furthermore, the films were exposed to several common compounds, including acetic acid, ethanol, CO2, H2S, NO and NO2 (500 ppm), for 15 min respectively. The resulting changes in fluorescence were compared with those observed when exposed to NH3 (20 ppm) for 15 min, to verify the selectivity of sensor. The reversibility of the N-GQDs-HNTs/PVA films was investigated by adding 100 μL (0.1 M) of NH3⋅H2O and 100 μL (0.1 M) of HCl successively onto the N-GQDs-HNTs/PVA film.

Live bighead carp samples were quickly transported to the laboratory in foam boxes filled with water and air from a local aquatic market in the Daxing district of Beijing. Subsequently, the fish were stunned by striking their heads with a wooden club once or twice. The methods of stunning and transportation used in the experiment complied with the guidelines recommended by the World Organisation for Animal Health (2012). After the fish were stunned, their heads were cut along the dorsal fin, and the gills were removed. The fish were subsequently cleaned and dried to serve as experimental samples.

The pre-prepared samples were immediately transferred into sealed containers with the fluorescent films and stored at 0 °C and 25 °C. The fluorescence intensity (λem = 440nm), total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) content and total viable count (TVC) of the samples stored at 0 °C and 25 °C were determined every 2 d and 4 h, respectively, until the values of TVB-N or TVC exceeded the spoilage threshold. Calibration curves relating the fluorescence intensity of the films and the TVB-N content of the samples were plotted.

The TVB-N content of the bighead carp samples was measured according to the Kjeldahl method (Zhang et al., 2022) via a semiautomatic Kjeldahl nitrogen determination apparatus (Tong Runyuan, KDY-9820, Beijing, China). TVC was determined using 3 M Aerobic Petrifilm™ (Trafalgar Scientific, Leicester, UK (SB01)) according to the standard plate count method (Zhang, Shen, & Luo, 2011).

The application of hybrid films in detecting salmon freshness followed the same process as described above. The processing of salmon samples was based on the previously proposed method (Jia et al., 2020).
A portable fluorescence spectrometer was used in the application of freshness detection of bighead carp (MONAISI, MNS-MFD-365, Suzhou, China).

As shown in Fig. 1a, the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites were well dispersed, and monodisperse N-GQDs were homogeneously attached to the HNTs, although a few GQD particles were detached. Fig. 1a insert shows that the N-GQDs were monodisperse and were distributed in the range from 1.26 nm to 5.71 nm with an average diameter of 2.84 ± 0.88 nm. The TEM results showed that the N-GQDs were successfully loaded on the HNTs.

FTIR was carried out to investigate the functional groups on the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 1b (black line), the absorption band of N-GQDs exhibited a peak centered at 3438 cm−1 that was attributed to the stretching vibration of the –OH moiety within –COOH groups (Rabeya et al., 2022). The peaks at 1643 cm−1 and 1325 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and C–NH2 moieties within the amide group, respectively. Other peaks at 3072 cm−1, 1576 cm−1, 1392 cm−1, 1182 cm−1 and 1095 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching of NH, CC, OH, CN and CO, respectively (Ju, Zhang, He, & Chen, 2014). Furthermore, the red line in Fig. 1b represents the FTIR spectrum of the HNTs, and absorption peaks related to stretching of the OH groups on the inner surface were observed at 3695 and 3624 cm−1. The absorption peaks at 3450 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 were attributed to the water adsorbed on the HNTs, and the peak at 1032 cm−1 was attributed to the vibration of the SiO network (Danyliuk, Tomaszewska, & Tatarchuk, 2020). The blue line in Fig. 1b represents the FTIR spectrum of the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite, and most of the characteristic peaks of the N-GQDs and HNTs were observed. Additionally, the absorption peaks in the spectrum of N-GQDs at 3438 cm−1, attributed to the –OH moiety within –COOH groups, and at 1325 cm−1, attributed to C–NH2, were absent from the N-GQDs/HNTs spectrum. The absorption peak at 3072 cm−1 in the N-GQDs spectrum attributed to NH shifted to 2929 cm−1 in the N-GQDs/HNTs spectrum. These changes suggested the formation of hydrogen bonds or electrostatic interactions caused by the interactions between the functional groups of N-GQDs and HNTs (Li et al., 2019).

XPS was used to analyze the surface elements and functional groups on the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite. The peaks attributed to C, N and O in the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite were observed at 284 eV, 399 eV, and 530 eV, respectively, with relative atomic percentages of 57.4%, 15.2%, and 27.4%, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 1c. In the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 1d), three peaks were identified at 283.6 eV, 284.9 eV and 286.8 eV, corresponding to CC, CO and CO, respectively (Zhu et al., 2017); the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1e) with peaks at 398.2 eV, 399.8 eV and 400.3 eV demonstrated the presence of pyridine N, –NH2 and amide groups (Arrigo et al., 2010); and the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1f) exhibited peaks at 529.7 eV and 530.6 eV, corresponding to O=C–C=O and CO, respectively (Arrigo et al., 2010). Compared to the XPS spectra of the HNTs (Fig. S1), the spectra of the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite indicated the presence of N-functional groups and a significant increase in C, which were attributed to the introduction of N-GQDs. The results from the XPS spectra were consistent with the FTIR findings, indicating the successful loading of N-GQDs on HNTs.

The HNTs consisted of siloxane groups (Si–O–Si), aluminol groups (Al–OH) and silanol groups (Si–OH) on the outer surface and edges, and Al–OH groups were abundant on the inner surface. The unique tubular structure of the HNTs resulted in a positive charge on the inner surface and a negative charge on the outer surface at neutral pH, providing numerous adsorption sites (Danyliuk et al., 2020).

The FTIR and XPS results suggested that the surface of the N-GQDs contained numerous carboxyl, amino and amide groups. A mechanism of N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite formation was speculated as follows: the positively charged amino and amide functional groups on the N-GQDs bound to the negatively charged outer surface of the HNTs through electrostatic interactions; moreover, hydrogen bonds were formed between the –COOH groups on the surface of the N-CQDs and the –OH groups on the inner and outer surfaces of the HNTs.

The zeta potentials and UV–vis spectra of the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites were investigated to confirm this proposed mechanism. The zeta potentials of the pure HNTs and N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites were − 24.6 mV and − 8.53 mV, respectively, as shown in Fig. S2. Obviously, the potential decreased after the HNTs and N-GQDs were combined, confirming the reasonableness of the electrostatic interactions (Hao et al., 2019). In summary, the TEM, FTIR, and XPS results indicated the successful formation of N-GQDs/HTNs nanocomposites, and the proposed formation mechanism was determined to be reasonable.

The surface of the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film was rough but lacked wrinkles with fine dot clusters, as shown in Fig. 2a. The cross-sections of the hybrid films displayed prominent and large lumps, as shown in Fig. 2b. These findings suggested that the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites were uniformly and homogenously integrated into the film. Furthermore, in the high-magnification SEM images of the surface and cross-sections (Fig. 2c and d), it was observed that the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites are not completely encapsulated by PVA. Many tubular structures were exposed on the surface and cross-section of the film, indicating that the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites were intricately intertwined with PVA, forming a network structure within the film.

In addition, the FTIR spectrum of the hybrid film revealed the characteristic peaks of the PVA film, as shown in Fig. 2e. The peaks at approximately 3252 cm−1, 2916 cm−1, 1709 cm−1, 1415 cm−1, and 1086 cm−1 were attributed to –OH, CH, CO, CC, and CO, respectively. There was no significant change in the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs FTIR spectrum compared to that of the pure PVA film, except the absorption peak at 3252 cm−1 in the PVA spectrum attributed to –OH shifted to a higher wavenumber of 3261 cm−1 in the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs spectrum. Similar observations have been reported in previous studies on PVA/HNTs films (Danyliuk et al., 2020). These findings were ascribed to the strengthening effect of hydrogen bonds formed between the –OH groups of PVA molecules and groups on the surface of N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites, including the –OH groups of the HNTs and the –NH2 groups of the N-GQDs (Ceylan et al., 2023; Yong et al., 2018).

Furthermore, XRD analyses of the hybrid films were conducted. The XRD data (Fig. 2f) revealed distinct crystal face peaks corresponding to PVA at 19.6° and 41.2°, indicating that doping of the nanomaterials did not affect the crystalline structure of PVA. Characteristic diffraction peaks of the HNTs and N-GQDs were not observed in the XRD spectrum, possibly due to their low content in the PVA film and their uniform dispersion in a randomly oriented manner (Mousa & Dong, 2020). Notably, the crystallinity of PVA was enhanced by the addition of GQDs and N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites but decreased by the addition of HNTs. It was concluded that the hydrogen bonds formed by the –NH2 moieties on the surface of the N-GQDs with the –OH groups of the PVA molecules enhanced the crystallinity of PVA (Alipoormazandarani, Ghazihoseini, & Nafchi, 2015). This speculation was also corroborated by an investigation of the mechanical properties of the hybrid films.

The optical properties of PVA-N-GQDs and PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid films with the same mass content of N-GQDs were investigated. In Fig. 3a, both UV–vis spectra show an absorption peak at 310 nm attributed to the n–π* transition of CO and CN (Xu et al., 2024). The films were transparent under sunlight, but the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film exhibited tiny spots formed by HNTs, as shown in the inset.

Both films emitted intense blue luminescence under UV light (365 nm), with an emission peak at 440 nm and an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film was much stronger than that of the PVA-N-GQDs film. Specifically, many “luminescent spots” due to the loading of N-GQDs on HNTs were observed on the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film.

Notably, a considerable redshift of the emission peaks was observed when the excitation wavelength was scanned from 290 nm to 400 nm for both the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film (Fig. S5a) and the PVA-N-GQDs film (Fig. S5b). This shift in fluorescence behavior was attributed to the dependence of the emission on the excitation wavelength of the N-GQDs (Ju et al., 2014).

The pH and N-GQDs/HNTs content in the as-prepared PVA films were optimized by comparing the fluorescence intensities. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites was minimally affected by pH within the range of 4 to 11. Hence, a neutral pH of approximately 7.0 was selected.

The fluorescence intensities of hybrid films fabricated with different N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites (Table S1), as shown in Fig. 3c, exhibited an initial increase followed by a decrease with increasing N-GQDs content. This decrease was due to the shorter distance between the N-GQDs with the excess loading of N-GQDs, increasing the nonradiative recombination and energy loss and thereby reducing the fluorescence intensity (Gong et al., 2023). Moreover, at the same N-GQDs concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid films significantly increased, reaching a maximum at 5 mg, and subsequently decreased with increasing HNTs due to the excess N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite in PVA, which led to diminished dispersion. Thus, the hybrid film b2 (N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposite with 2.25 mg of N-GQDs and 5 mg of HNTs) was chosen for study.

The experimental findings suggested that the HNTs served as appropriate carrier materials, preserving the distinctive optical properties of N-GQDs. The intensity of the emission significantly increased in the hybrid film, as expected because the N-GQDs were dispersed on the surfaces of the HNTs through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, effectively reducing the AIE caused by the aggregation of the N-GQDs in PVA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties, MC, WVP and WCA
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical properties, MC, WVP and WCA of the hybrid films and control films were analyzed, and the results are listed in Table 1 . The thickness of the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film increased from 48.00 ± 5.80 μm (for pure PVA) to 50.00 ± 0.50 μm, indicating that the thickness of the films was not significantly affected by the addition of fillers. Despite extensive studies suggesting that the use of HNTs as fillers can enhance the mechanical characteristics of polymer films, PVA-HNTs exhibited inferior mechanical performance (Lin et al., 2023). The decrease in mechanical characteristics was attributed to the poor interfacial compatibility resulting from a lack of sufficient adhesion between PVA and HNTs (Fujii, Nakagaito, Takagi, & Yonekura, 2014). The TS and EB of the PVA-N-GQDs film and the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film were greater than those of the pure PVA film and PVA-HNTs film, which was attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the abundant –NH2 groups on the surface of the N-GQDs and the –OH groups on the PVA molecular chains. The results of the mechanical property analysis aligned with the FTIR observations of the films.

The incorporation of N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites led to an increase in the MC density of the PVA films, which was ascribed to the pronounced hydrophilicity of the surface functional groups, including –COOH and –OH groups.

The WCA is frequently employed to assess the wettability of films. As shown in Table 1, the hydrophilicity of the hybrid films improved compared to that of the pure PVA film, as evidenced by the decreasing WCA values, which decreased to <20° (Liu et al., 2023). The tubular channels and network structure facilitated by HNTs, along with the substantial presence of hydrophilic hydroxyl groups in N-GQDs, contributed to an enhanced hydrophilicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of the hybrid film
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 3d, upon the introduction of ammonia into the hybrid film, a noticeable decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed. Photographs of the hybrid film, both without and with 500 ppm ammonia under UV light (Fig. 3d insert), depicted the transformation of the blue emission from vibrant to dim.

The calibration curve for the hybrid films depicting the relationship between F0/F and ammonia concentration in the range of 20–500 ppm (Fig. 3e) demonstrated excellent linearity. Here, F0 represents the initial fluorescence intensity, and F represents the intensity after ammonia addition. The hybrid film showed comparable sensitivity, with a large calibration curve slope, and excellent linearity, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.98. The linear detection range of the hybrid film for ammonia was 20 to 500 ppm, with an LOD of 0.63 ppm, as calculated by means of the formula 3σ/x (Ali, Hassan, Hassan, & El-Wekil, 2020).

The stability, selectivity and reversibility, which are crucial assessment criteria, of the sensors were investigated, and the results are depicted in Fig. S6. The fluorescence intensity exhibited a minimal variation of only 7% over seven days within an indoor environment, underscoring the remarkable stability of the film. The impacts of potentially competing volatile compounds, including CO2, NO, NO2, H2S, ethanol, and acetic acid, were thoroughly investigated (Fig. S6b). Remarkably, the hybrid film demonstrated superior selectivity for ammonia in comparison to other substances. Furthermore, the reversibility of the hybrid films was analyzed. As shown in Fig. S6c, the fluorescence intensity did not return to its initial value. The HNTs-based composites exhibited a strong retention ability for ammonia owing to their chemical nature and pore structure (Noskov et al., 2022), which may explain the poor reversibility of the hybrid films. In future research, prioritizing the resolution of technical bottlenecks related to the reversibility of hybrid films for ammonia detection should be our focus. For example, enhancing the reversibility of processes such as adsorption and desorption of ammonia through surface modification could improve the reusability of hybrid films, thereby reducing usage costs.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-GQDs/HNTs toward ammonia was investigated in our research. As shown in Fig. S7a, the UV–vis spectra of the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid films without and with ammonia were unchanged (Ezati et al., 2023), indicating that ammonia did not chemically react with the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites to form ground-state compounds. The FTIR spectra of the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites without and with ammonia also showed no new peaks (Fig. S7b), further confirming the above analysis. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching in the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites induced by ammonia did not occur via SQE.

A typical Stern–Volmer diagram (Fig. 3e) was constructed to further confirm the quenching process in the steady state:
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
where F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites, F is the fluorescence intensity observed after the addition of ammonia, K
SV is the quenching Stern–Volmer constant, [Q] is the concentration of ammonia, K
q is the bimolecular rate constant and
τ 0
is the lifetime of the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites without ammonia. In general, the K
q for SQE exceeds 2 × 1010 L·mol− 1·s− 1 (Cui, Song, Wang, & Song, 2015). The K
q values were 1.91 × 106, as calculated from the results in Fig. 3e, indicating that SQE can be ruled out, which is consistent with the results obtained from the UV–vis spectra and FTIR.

The time-resolved fluorescence decay was measured to investigate the interaction between the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites and ammonia in the excited state. The fluorescence decay lifetimes of the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites without and with ammonia were fitted with a triexponential decay equation (Table S2) with values of 4.63 ns and 4.05 ns, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3f. The significant decrease in the fluorescence lifetime suggested the occurrence of interactions between the donor and acceptor in the excited state (Ghosh, Chatterjee, & Prasad, 2015). Additionally, energy transfer quenching was impossible in this study because electron-donating ammonia was not photoexcited.

In summary, photoinduced electron transfer (PET) was determined to be the mechanism of dynamic fluorescence quenching of the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites toward ammonia. GQDs are known for their electron- and energy-accepting properties (Li, Chen, Gooding, & Liu, 2019). In our research, the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites acted as electron acceptors due to the presence of electron-accepting groups (—COOH and —CONH2) on the surface of the GQDs (Travlou, Secor, & Bandosz, 2017), whereas ammonia contributed to electron donation through the lone pair electrons on the N atom, which led to the quenching of the N-GQDs/HNTs nanocomposites upon photoexcitation.

The PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film was applied to assess the freshness of bighead carp samples during storage at both room temperature (25 °C) and under refrigeration (0 °C) to investigate its practicality. Common international indicators for evaluating the freshness of fish, TVB-N and TVC, were investigated as references. The suggested reference value for TVB-N in evaluating the freshness of fish is <20 mg/100 g (Kostaki, Giatrakou, Savvaidis, & Kontominas, 2009), and the limit for TVC in fish is 7 log10 CFU/g (Taliadourou, Papadopoulos, Domvridou, Savvaidis, & Kontominas, 2003).

The changes in the freshness of bighead carp stored at 25 °C are illustrated in Fig. 4a. The TVB-N value increased from the initial value of 9.10 mg/100 g to 23.45 mg/100 g after 24 h, surpassing the suggested TVB-N reference value for freshwater fish. Similarly, the initial TVC for fresh fish was 4.89 log10 CFU/g, and it increased up to 7.53 log10 CFU/g after 20 h of storage, exceeding the TVC limit for freshwater fish. These results indicated that the samples had spoiled after approximately 20–24 h at 25 °C.

For bighead carp stored at 0 °C, as shown in Fig. 4c, the initial TVB-N value for fresh fish was 8.75 mg/100 g, and it increased to 21.14 mg/100 g after 16 d. Additionally, the TVC reached 7.14 log10 CFU/g after 12 d of storage, starting from the initial value of 4.72 log10 CFU/g for fresh fish. The limits for TVB-N and TVC were exceeded at 16 d and 12 d, respectively, indicating that the spoilage of bighead carp occurred after approximately 12–16 d at 0 °C.

After the storage of bighead carp samples at both 25 °C and 0 °C, the duration to reach the TVB-N threshold consistently lagged behind the time necessary to reach the TVC threshold because the formation of TVB-N inherently trailed the growth of microbial populations. These results are consistent with previous research findings (Zhang et al., 2022).

Calibration curves correlating TVB-N with the fluorescence intensity of the hybrid films are plotted in Fig. 4b and Fig. 4d. The hybrid films exhibited remarkable linearity at both 25 °C and 0 °C. Additionally, the limits of detection (LODs) for these hybrid films were 9.20 mg/100 g and 8.89 mg/100 g, respectively, which were below the recommended TVB-N threshold of 10 mg/100 g for fresh freshwater fish (Kostaki et al., 2009); moreover, the detection range of hybrid films extended to approximately 25 mg/100 g, surpassing the TVB-N level associated with spoiled freshwater fish. As shown in Fig. 4e-f, photos of the hybrid films under UV light (365 nm) were taken over time. The color of the hybrid films under UV light gradually shifted from an intensely bright blue to a paler blue, eventually becoming colorless as storage time elapsed.

In addition, the application of hybrid film was investigated in salmon freshness detection. As shown in Fig. S8, the hybrid films exhibited good linearity at room temperature and refrigerated temperature. These results indicated that the hybrid films can be used to effectively and quantitatively monitor the freshness of fish throughout the entire storage period through the measurement of fluorescence intensity. This approach enables real-time and portable monitoring of fish freshness via the use of a portable fluorescence sensor alongside the hybrid films.

Our work was compared to several previously reported studies, as presented in Table S3. In our work, a pH-insensitive N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film was used as a signal indicator for ammonia, reducing interference from coexisting acidic volatile compounds during freshness detection. Moreover, compared with those of the materials reported in the table, the performance of the hybrid film fabricated in this study, including its detection range and LOD, was comparable or superior.

In summary, we developed an ammonia-sensitive fluorescent sensor based on a PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film, which was successfully applied to determine the freshness of bighead carp. The N-GQDs were loaded on the surfaces of the HNTs through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, effectively relieving their aggregation behavior; thus, the PVA-N-GQDs/HNTs hybrid film exhibited significantly improved fluorescence. The fluorescence of the hybrid film was quenched by ammonia via the PET effect. The hybrid film demonstrated good linearity between the fluorescence intensity and ammonia concentration in the range of 20 ppm to 500 ppm, with an LOD of 0.63 ppm. In addition, the fluorescence response of the hybrid film was linearly related to the TVB-N content accumulated in bighead carp during storage at different temperatures (25 °C and 0 °C). The proposed hybrid film demonstrated easy preparation, portable operation and excellent sensing performance, and it has shown promising potential for real-time use by integration with electrodevices in the field of freshness monitoring.

Jiaran Zhang: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jie Yu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Hao Yin: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Zhixin Jia: Data curation, Formal analysis. Ce Shi: Funding acquisition, Validation. Yuntao Yue: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been financially supported by the Youth Scientific Research and Innovation Project of Beijing University of Civil Engineering and Architecture (X23023) and the Golden Bridge Project Seed Funding of Beijing Association for Science and Technology (Z23151).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.139734.
